FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes Years 2–4 follow-up data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 5.1 release, 21 U.S. research sites, baseline ages 9–10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical causal mediation analysis examining caffeine use as mediator between gaming duration/problems (Year 2) and sleep outcomes (Year 4).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2025).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere in the paper. All exposure variables are gaming-only: daily gaming duration (hours/day) and VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, a generic gaming addiction scale). Platform is unspecified (single-player or online multiplayer video games). VGAQ is explicitly a gaming measure adapted from the Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale but measures gaming, not SM/phone use. No phone time, no SM variable, no named platform, no texting, no SMAQ/MPIQ. Step 2: No composite is analyzed; the exposure is gaming-only. Under binding clarification, VGAQ and generic gaming duration without explicit mobile/phone modality fail IC2.

notes: Paper is entirely about gaming (unspecified platform), caffeine use, and sleep. No smartphone or social media variable measured or analyzed.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes three consecutive waves (Years 2–4) of the U.S. ABCD Study (ABCD data release 6.0, https://doi.org/10.82525/jy7n-g441), approximately 7,260 participants across 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: multilevel linear mixed-effects model (ADHD → VGAQ over time) plus parallel mediation model (ADHD → psychosocial mediators → VGAQ).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Frontiers in Psychiatry (2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere in the paper. The primary outcome is VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), which measures gaming addiction symptoms for video games with platform unspecified. The predictors are ADHD diagnosis (KSADS-COMP) and psychosocial mediators (prosocial peer involvement, school involvement, family conflict, parental monitoring, etc.). No phone time, no social media variable, no named SM platform, no SMAQ/MPIQ appears anywhere in the main text, tables, or supplementary material. Step 2: No composite analyzed; exposure is VGAQ only. Under binding clarification, VGAQ (generic video game addiction questionnaire, unspecified platform) fails IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable, which is absent here.

notes: Paper focuses entirely on ADHD → gaming addiction (VGAQ) with psychosocial mediators. No smartphone or social media variable is measured or analyzed.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, longitudinal data collected 2016–2022, n=9,474, baseline ages 9–10, 21 research sites, ABCD Data Release 5.1.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) of screen time and social media trajectories plus multivariable linear regression of trajectory groups on anxiety/depression t-scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in BMC Public Health (2025/2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Social media use is analyzed separately as a secondary exposure with its own GBTM trajectory groups and beta coefficients against anxiety/depression (individual-trait outcomes). Table 4 reports numeric estimates: Group 2 (Gradual Increase to Moderate Use) β = -0.30 (95% CI: -0.58, -0.03) for anxiety; Groups 3–5 show β = 1.03, 1.08, 1.49 for depression (all p ≤ 0.014). Time on social media is a qualifying SM-specific variable per Step 1; anxiety/depression (CBCL DSM-5 t-scores) are individual-trait-level outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 4 reports standardized beta coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for social media trajectory groups predicting anxiety and depression t-scores. Multiple numeric results reported for the SM ↔ mental health relationship.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analysis with numeric results (betas, CIs, p-values) for SM → anxiety/depression relationship; quantitative results present throughout main tables.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because social media is used as IV (predictor of anxiety/depression), not as DV. The paper predicts anxiety/depression from SM trajectory groups. Condition (b) is not met.
notes: The general screen time composite (hours on TV, texting, gaming, social media combined) is analyzed as a primary exposure (IV → anxiety/depression), and social media is also analyzed separately as a secondary exposure (IV → anxiety/depression). Both analyses use SM/screen as IV, so EC7 cannot apply. Paper qualifies via the separate social media → mental health analysis in Table 4.
